notes: Owns policy evaluation, approvals, model selection, capability routing, and sub-agent policy.
Registers hook-based approval pauses for risky tool calls.
Owns queue submission, status, retries, artifact registration, and worker callbacks.
Uses OpenClaw sessions_send and sessions_spawn only.
Never sends Telegram messages directly from a worker.
Compiles deterministic office job specs.
Delegates execution to adapter-backed external workers.
Does not manipulate Office files in-process.
Wraps approved-root file access with deterministic read, write, patch, copy, move, and preview flows.
Read-only file operations can run without approval; destructive operations require approval unless the root is pre-authorized.
Brokers browser work onto OpenClaw's managed browser stack rather than creating a second browser runtime.
Pairs deterministic browser job specs with approval-aware execution for privileged browser actions.
Compiles deterministic host-observation and desktop-control job specs.
Delegates execution to a separate desktop-host worker and never automates the machine inside the Gateway process.
Forms the base control plane for future file, browser, app, and publish workflows on the current device.
Monitors CPU, memory, disk, network, battery. Manages processes. Reports hardware inventory.
Auto-detects Ollama and LM Studio runtimes at their default ports.
Routes requests to the optimal local model based on requested tier (haiku/sonnet/opus).
Falls back to adjacent tiers when exact match is unavailable.
Provides RAG indexing and querying backed by local embeddings.
Supports async batch inference with status polling.
Manages recurring job schedules using cron expressions or fixed intervals.
Evaluates threshold alert rules against metric paths extracted from job outputs.
Uses an in-memory store for schedule and alert records; no SQLite dependency.
Supports scope groups for organizational isolation of schedules.

plugins.role: open interpreter bridge for multi-step automation
notes: Orchestrates Open Interpreter for complex multi-step automation tasks.
Requires user approval before any code execution due to arbitrary code execution risk.
Supports Python, JavaScript, and shell code execution via direct subprocess spawning.
Session tracking allows monitoring of long-running interpreter tasks.
Captures microphone audio via ffmpeg or sox and stores as WAV artifacts.
Transcribes audio artifacts using Whisper STT (whisper.cpp or faster-whisper).
Synthesises speech via Piper TTS (cross-platform) or Windows SAPI (PowerShell fallback).
Manages persistent wake word detection sessions with configurable keyword and sensitivity.
Scans running processes against a configurable whitelist and flags suspicious activity.
Audits active network connections and identifies connections to known malicious ports.
Provides SHA-256 file integrity baselines and change detection.
Manages Windows Firewall rules via netsh for inbound and outbound traffic control.
Lockdown mode kills non-whitelisted processes, blocks outbound traffic, and locks the screen.
Manages the full lifecycle of autonomous agents: registration, starting, stepping, pausing, resuming, and configuring.
Uses an in-memory AgentRuntime and AgentMemoryStore for short-term and long-term memory management.
Supports multiple trigger kinds: manual, schedule, event, and threshold.
Approval is required for agent start and resume when configured gates are active.
Lists, creates, and updates calendar events via the calendar worker adapter.
Finds free time slots across multiple attendees within a search window.
Generates structured meeting preparation briefs including key topics, recommended agenda, and attendee context.
Event creation requires approval; updates are conditional based on scope; read operations require no approval.
Searches inbox using Gmail query syntax (from:, subject:, label:, free text).
Reads full message body, headers, and attachment metadata by message ID.
Creates drafts in Gmail without sending; draft_id is returned for later use.
Sending always requires user approval to prevent accidental outbound email.
Applies or removes Gmail labels conditionally; removal from INBOX is guarded by approval.
Lists threads grouped by thread_id with participant and recency metadata.
Searches news sources and the web for articles and information about companies or topics.
Scrapes structured profile data from company pages, LinkedIn profiles, and job postings.
Monitors web pages for content changes across runs using stable page identifiers and content hashes.
Enriches contact records with role, company size, industry, and LinkedIn data sourced from the web.
Tracks job postings at target companies and filters by keyword relevance for talent intelligence.
Gathers competitive intelligence including key facts, recent news, and product/team signals.
Manages an in-memory CRM pipeline with stages: prospect → qualified → contacted → meeting → proposal → negotiation → won | lost | parked.
Supports full-text search across contact name, company, tags, and notes.
Generates pipeline digest reports including hot leads (score > 70 in late stages) and stale contact detection.
Contact creation and mutation operations are conditional-approval; read and digest operations require no approval.

plugins.role: document intelligence and automotive safety compliance
notes: Ingests PDF, DOCX, TXT, and MD documents extracting text, structure, and tables.
Extracts and classifies NDA/contract clauses with risk assessment (low, medium, high, critical).
Analyzes compliance against ISO 26262, ASPICE, IEC 61508, and ISO 21434 with gap reporting.
Compares two document versions and classifies changes by significance (minor, moderate, major).
Generates structured reports from templates (evidence gap, compliance summary, NDA analysis, proposal, custom) in DOCX, PDF, or Markdown.

boundary_rules: OpenClaw remains the source of truth for sessions, routing, channel connections, and browser primitives.
Workers communicate back through the jobs callback route and never directly to chat channels.
Approval is required for external dispatch, unrestricted python execution, privileged browser actions, and destructive file edits.
Desktop and device automation runs in a separate host worker, not inside the Gateway process.
